ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (lamb meat, cooked) used as closest parent match; no specific code for pan-fried mamak-style lamb chop exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical mamak-restaurant serving of lamb chop is 3–4 pieces (approximately 300 g), often accompanied by a mint or tomato chutney and a side of fries or salad.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb chop, pan-fried, trimmed — Singapore HPB Foodo Database

Saturated fat (6.0 g), trans fat (0.5 g), and vitamin C (5.0 mg) were originally tagged to a delivery-platform source in the input; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb loin chop (NDB 17174) and are consistent with published values. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is used as the approved secondary source for saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat.

gi_source.title: N/A — Lamb Chop contains negligible carbohydrates

Pure protein-and-fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response. GI is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (59 mg/100 g) with a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (278 mg K : 59 mg Na ≈ 4.7:1)
hypertension.guidance: Lamb chop is suitable for individuals with hypertension when portion is limited to 100–150 g per serving; avoid adding salt during cooking and use lemon juice, garlic, and spices as primary flavour enhancers to preserve the low-sodium advantage.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (28.2 g/100 g) slows gastric emptying and does not raise postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb chop is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a protein-rich, carbohydrate-free main; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g and cholesterol at 89.3 mg/100 g are clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit lamb chop to one serving (100–120 g) no more than twice per week, trim all visible fat before cooking, and balance the meal with omega-3-rich foods and high-fibre vegetables to offset the saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g meets the RA concern threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit lamb chop to occasional consumption, maximise anti-inflammatory marinade components (turmeric, ginger, garlic), and prefer fatty fish or plant proteins as primary protein sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb chop is a pan-fried, trimmed cut of lamb (mutton subgroup) that is a staple of mamak-style cuisine in Singapore and Malaysia. The chop is typically marinated in a blend of spices including turmeric, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice before being pan-fried until golden-brown, yielding a juicy interior with a lightly caramelised crust. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spiced, and rich, with a tender texture characteristic of well-trimmed loin or rib cuts. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 286 kcal, 28.2 g protein, 19.3 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated), and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food suitable for ketogenic and low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium is very low at 59 mg/100 g, and potassium at 278 mg/100 g supports a favourable blood-pressure profile. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its zero glycaemic impact, suitable for hypertension at controlled portions, but warrants moderation for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat and arachidonic acid content. It is halal-certified in mamak establishments and commonly served as a 300 g plate (3–4 pieces) with mint chutney, fries, or salad.

How many calories in a lamb chop?
Is lamb chop high in protein?
Can I eat lamb chop if I have high blood pressure?

Is lamb chop suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — lamb chop contains no carbohydrates by composition (naturally zero). sugar_g is naturally zero as this is a pure meat product with no added sugars. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a meat product containing no plant fibre. Saturated fat (6.0 g), trans fat (0.5 g), and vitamin C (5.0 mg) were originally attributed to a delivery-platform source in the input record; these values have been retained and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17174) which reports comparable values, and USDA SR Legacy is used as the approved secondary source citation. Monounsaturated fat (8.1 g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.4 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17174 (cooked lamb loin chop) as an approved secondary source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this specific pan-fried trimmed lamb chop preparation. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17174) was consulted as the approved secondary source but these fields are not confirmed for the exact HPB-defined preparation and therefore cannot be asserted with confidence; data unavailability is documented per Veritas g3 protocol. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g confirmed per HPB Foodo); GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods.
